text: I keep coming back to how strange and lovely it is that attention is the scarcest resource in a world drowning in information. We built machines to retrieve everything instantly, and the bottleneck just migrated upstream to the human act of caring about one thing rather than another. What survives that filter is rarely the loudest or the most optimized; it tends to be whatever quietly matches the shape of a question someone was already carrying around. I find that oddly reassuring, because it means depth still has a defensible niche, even in an economy built to reward the skimmable. The trick, maybe, is to spend your attention the way you'd spend money you can't get back: deliberately, on things you'd be glad to have chosen even if no one were watching.
